source.title: Fire and Explosions at Philadelphia Energy Solutions Refinery Hydrofluoric Acid Alkylation Unit

event.top_event: Rupture of steel pipe elbow with release of hydrofluoric acid and hydrocarbons
event.incident_type: Loss of Containment with Fire and Explosions
actions_taken: Rapid Acid Deinventory (RAD) system activated at 4:02:37 a.m., draining 339,000 pounds of HF to RAD drum
Emergency response team mobilized and positioned fire engine
Fixed fire monitors activated on south side of unit
Shift supervisor manually activated water pump for HF mitigation cannons at approximately 4:39 a.m.
Isolation valves manually closed at unit battery limits to stop hydrocarbon and steam flow
HF in RAD drum neutralized with base chemical reaction from August 7 to August 27, 2019
event.summary: On June 21, 2019 at 4:00:16 a.m., a steel pipe elbow with high nickel and copper content ruptured in the HF alkylation unit discharge piping of pump P14-B. The rupture released approximately 5,239 pounds of HF and 676,000 pounds of hydrocarbons, creating a large vapor cloud composed of approximately 95% propane and 2.5% HF. The vapor cloud ignited at 4:02:06 a.m., two minutes after the initial release. Three secondary explosions occurred, with the largest at 4:22 a.m. when vessel V-1 ruptured violently, ejecting fragments weighing up to 38,000 pounds across the Schuylkill River and into the refinery. The fire was extinguished on June 22 at approximately 8:30 a.m. The incident resulted in an estimated property damage loss of $750 million, ranking it as the third-largest refinery loss worldwide since 1974. Five workers and one firefighter sustained minor injuries. The root cause was the rupture of a steel piping component with high nickel and copper content that had corroded from HF exposure and thinned faster than adjacent piping components with lower nickel and copper content.
recommendations: EPA: Develop a program prioritizing inspections of refinery HF alkylation units under EPA's National Compliance Initiative, verifying compliance with API RP 751 including special emphasis inspection programs for accelerated corrosion, protection of safety-critical safeguards from fire and explosion hazards, and installation of remotely-operated emergency isolation valves

EPA: Revise 40 C.F.R. Part 68 to require new and existing petroleum refineries with HF alkylation units to conduct safer technology and alternatives analysis (STAA) and evaluate practicability of inherently safer technology (IST) every 5 years
EPA: Initiate prioritization to evaluate whether hydrofluoric acid is a High-Priority Substance for risk evaluation under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation if warranted
API: Update API RP 751 to require protection of critical safeguards and control system components from fire and explosion hazards including radiant heat and flying projectiles

API: Update API RP 751 to require installation of remotely-operated emergency isolation valves on inlets and outlets of HF-containing and hydrocarbon-containing vessels meeting defined threshold quantities
ASTM International: Revise ASTM A234 to incorporate supplementary requirements for piping used in HF service as defined in ASTM A106 version 19a

hazards.name: High Temperature and Pressure Process Conditions

threats.name: Corrosion of carbon steel piping with high nickel and copper content in HF service
Pipe elbow wall thinning from accelerated HF corrosion
Rupture of corroded pipe elbow under operating pressure
Ignition of released hydrocarbon vapor cloud
Failure of water spray HF mitigation system due to fire damage and loss of control system communication
Inability to remotely isolate large hydrocarbon sources downstream of pipe rupture
Secondary explosions caused by fire in unit

consequences.name: Release of hydrofluoric acid to atmosphere
Release of pressurized hydrocarbons to atmosphere
Large fire in HF alkylation unit
Violent rupture of V-1 vessel with ejection of large fragments
Flying projectiles impacting equipment and structures
Severe damage to HF alkylation unit and refinery equipment
Potential off-site HF exposure to surrounding community

controls.name: Mechanical Integrity Program - Piping Inspection
controls.lod_basis: Industry standard practice for process safety management

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, published industry standards and recommended practices did not require refineries to conduct 100% component inspection of carbon steel piping in HF service to identify any piping components corroding and thinning faster than others
The ruptured elbow had a minimum wall thickness of 0.011 inch, which is less than 7% of the PES default retirement thickness of 0.180 inch
The elbow that ruptured and the other piping components within its circuit were installed around 1973

controls.name: Material Specification Compliance - ASTM A234 Grade WPB
controls.barrier_role: Ensure piping materials are suitable for HF service

supporting_text: The original specification for this piping required fittings such as elbows to be constructed of ASTM A234, Grade WPB, weight to match pipe
The failed steel elbow had a high nickel and copper content
Carbon steel with high nickel and copper content is known within the industry to corrode faster from contact with HF than carbon steel with lower nickel and copper content

controls.name: Rapid Acid Deinventory (RAD) System
controls.barrier_role: Quickly remove HF inventory from unit during loss-of-containment event

supporting_text: At 4:02:37 a.m., the control room operator activated the RAD system, which drained about 339,000 pounds (43,260 U.S. gallons) of hydrofluoric acid from the unit to the RAD drum
This activation of the RAD system occurred two minutes and 21 seconds after the release started, and 31 seconds after ignition
This two minute and 21 second response time was within the PES target operator response time of two minutes and 30 seconds
The RAD system was designed to be manually activated by an operator, and could be activated from three separate locations

controls.name: Water Spray HF Mitigation System
controls.barrier_role: Suppress released HF through vapor suppression using elevated water cannons
controls.lod_basis: API RP 751 requirement for HF alkylation units

supporting_text: At 4:12 a.m., the control room operator tried to remotely turn on the water pumps that fed the elevated HF mitigation water cannons, which were designed to reduce airborne HF through vapor suppression, but the water pumps did not turn on

The control system communication to the water pumps had failed at 4:02:06 a.m. (time of ignition) and a backup power system in the unit—the uninterruptible power supply (UPS)—also failed nine seconds later at 4:02:15
Field operator 1 tried to walk to the water pumps to manually turn them on, but the operator could not get to the pumps because the fire made that area too hot to enter

At about 4:39 a.m., the alkylation unit shift supervisor entered the alkylation unit in firefighting protective gear called bunker gear and manually turned on the water pump that supplied the HF mitigation water cannons

controls.name: Remote Emergency Isolation Valves on Hydrocarbon Sources
controls.barrier_role: Isolate large hydrocarbon sources downstream of pipe rupture to stop jet flame impingement
controls.lod_basis: Process safety best practice for HF alkylation units

supporting_text: Although there were emergency isolation valves on the suction (inlet) of each of the P-14 pumps, there were no emergency isolation valves downstream of the pumps, between the pumps and columns T-6 and T-7
Those large hydrocarbon sources downstream of the failed elbow could not be remotely or automatically isolated

Based on the thinning of the V-1 vessel steel, the CSB concluded that a jet flame from the ruptured elbow impinged upon the bottom of the V-1 vessel, causing the steel to stretch and thin until the vessel ruptured

controls.name: RAGAGEP Compliance and Equipment Verification
controls.barrier_role: Ensure equipment remains safe when new safety information is published in RAGAGEP documents
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulatory requirements

supporting_text: The seminal research presented in the 2003 NACE paper 03651 Specification for Carbon Steel Materials for Hydrofluoric Acid Alkylation Units directly led to changes in industry guidance quantifying the levels of nickel and copper in steel that could be considered safe for use in HF alkylation units

Prior to the incident, API RP 751, Sunoco, and PES did not effectively respond to these advancements in industry knowledge by ensuring the safety of existing facilities through requiring all carbon steel piping circuit components to be inspected

Both the OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations require companies to determine that their equipment is designed, maintained, inspected, tested, and operating in a safe manner when new safety information is discovered and published in RAGAGEP documents

controls.name: Protection of Safety-Critical Safeguards from Fire and Explosion Hazards
controls.barrier_role: Protect control system components and power supplies from fire and explosion damage
controls.lod_basis: Process safety best practice for HF alkylation units

supporting_text: The control system communication to the water pumps had failed at 4:02:06 a.m. (time of ignition) and a backup power system in the unit—the uninterruptible power supply (UPS)—also failed nine seconds later at 4:02:15

The damage to the PES water spray HF mitigation system demonstrates that active safeguards—or safeguards that require a person or technology to trigger their activation—have the potential to fail in major incidents involving fires and explosions

controls.name: Inherently Safer Design Evaluation
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate and implement safer alternatives to HF alkylation technology
controls.lod_basis: Process safety best practice and emerging regulatory requirements

supporting_text: Technologies are being developed that could be a safer alternative to HF and sulfuric acid alkylation, including composite ionic liquid catalyst alkylation technology, solid acid catalyst alkylation technology, and the new ionic liquid acid catalyst alkylation technology developed by Chevron, which is now operating at commercial scale at Chevron's Salt Lake City refinery
There is currently no Federal regulatory requirement for petroleum refineries to evaluate inherently safer design strategies to reduce the risk of serious accidental releases

controls.name: Process Monitoring and Alarm Systems
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal process conditions and alert operators

supporting_text: The alarms that activated in the control system in the first few seconds were: Vibration spike on the offline spare depropanizer reflux pump, P14-B; Low flow alarm on the propane stripper feed line; Vibration spike on the running depropanizer reflux pump, P14-A

Soon after the control room operator acknowledged the flow and vibration alarms, the control room operator saw a continuous and rapid succession of alarms from various equipment and sensors in the unit, including hydrofluoric acid-in-air detectors

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
notes.extraction_notes: This incident involved a catastrophic failure of a corroded pipe elbow in an HF alkylation unit. The primary failure mechanism was accelerated corrosion of carbon steel with high nickel and copper content, which was not adequately managed through inspection programs. Multiple secondary barriers failed or were ineffective: the water spray mitigation system lost control system communication and backup power during the fire, and remote isolation valves were not installed on large hydrocarbon sources downstream of the rupture. The RAD system successfully functioned when manually activated by the operator, demonstrating the critical importance of human intervention in emergency response. The incident highlights significant gaps in RAGAGEP compliance, mechanical integrity programs, and safeguard reliability in HF alkylation units. Communication challenges were noted when field operator 3's radio transmission was garbled. The organization failed to implement updated industry knowledge regarding material specifications for HF service after the 2003 NACE paper was published. Equipment inadequacy is evidenced by the lack of remote isolation valves and the vulnerability of the water mitigation system to fire damage.
